name: University of Cambridge
notes: The UK's broadest and most prestigious general engineering degree; all students take the same first two years before specialising in Year 3 into Mechanical, Civil, Electrical, Aerospace, or Information Engineering. ENGAA entrance assessment required; interview at college. A* in Maths and Physics A-levels expected.

name: University of Oxford
notes: Oxford's integrated Engineering Science degree covers all engineering disciplines for the first two years; students specialise from Year 3. Interview required. PAT (Physics Aptitude Test) required at application. Mathematics and Physics A-levels essential; A* expected in Mathematics.

name: Imperial College London
notes: Imperial offers broad Engineering entry via H100; students specialise in Year 1 into specific departments. A* in Mathematics expected. Strong employer links to aerospace, energy and technology sectors. No interview for most Engineering courses.

name: University of Warwick
notes: Warwick Engineering combines rigorous mathematics with practical design. Strong employer links to Jaguar Land Rover, Rolls-Royce and Tesla (Warwick Manufacturing Group WMG). A*AA required; Mathematics and Physics essential.

name: Loughborough University
notes: Loughborough is consistently ranked among the UK's best engineering universities. World-renowned for sporting and automotive engineering links. Strong Formula SAE and aerospace connections; graduates placed at McLaren, Williams, Red Bull F1 and Rolls-Royce. Excellent graduate employment record.

name: University of Bristol
notes: Bristol's Faculty of Engineering includes broad entry options. Particularly strong in aerospace (Rolls-Royce, Airbus adjacency) and civil engineering. Mathematics and Physics required at A-level.

name: University of Bath
notes: Bath consistently achieves among the UK's highest graduate employment rates in engineering. Integrated placement year (Semester 5) gives practical industrial experience, typically at top engineering companies. Mathematics A-level required.

name: University of Southampton
notes: Southampton Engineering is world-leading in aeronautics, astronautics and marine engineering. The university operates its own wind tunnel and ship hydrodynamics laboratory. Strong links to Lloyd's Register, BAE Systems and QinetiQ.

name: University of Manchester
notes: Manchester Engineering benefits from the graphene discovery (Geim & Novoselov) and has strong chemical and materials engineering research. The National Graphene Institute provides exceptional facilities. Mathematics required; Physics recommended.

name: University of Nottingham
notes: Nottingham Engineering has strong Rolls-Royce connections (the company is based nearby and has a university research partnership). The Manufacturing Technology Centre at Coventry is a key industry partner. Mathematics A-level required; Physics strongly recommended.

name: University of Sheffield
notes: Sheffield's AMRC (Advanced Manufacturing Research Centre) with Boeing is one of the UK's leading engineering research centres. Strong links to aerospace (Boeing, Rolls-Royce) and automotive industries. Excellent hands-on engineering culture. Mathematics required at A-level.

name: University of Leeds
notes: Leeds Engineering covers a broad range with particular strengths in civil, mechanical and chemical engineering. Strong industrial placement culture. Mathematics required at A-level; Physics recommended.
